`@nospecialize` does not work when attached to unused parameters in a function signature (i.e. those named `_`).
More generally, unused parameters, unless both named and used with `@no_specialize` will generate specializations even if they are unable to affect the result of a method invocation.

# Description
Consider the following three function definitions
```julia
f(a, b, _) = a + b / 2

f(1, 2, 3)
f(1, 2, 3.0)
f(1, 2, 3.0 + im)

g(a, b, @nospecialize _) = a + b / 2

g(1, 2, 3)
g(1, 2, 3.0)
g(1, 2, 3.0 + im)

h(a, b, @nospecialize _x) = a + b / 2

h(1, 2, 3)
h(1, 2, 3.0)
h(1, 2, 3.0 + im)
```
If we inspect the specializations for these methods, we find the following

```julia_repl
julia> Base.specializations(methods(f)[1])
Base.MethodSpecializations(svec(MethodInstance for f(::Int64, ::Int64, ::Int64), MethodInstance for f(::Int64, ::Int64, ::Float64),
 MethodInstance for f(::Int64, ::Int64, ::ComplexF64), nothing, nothing, nothing, nothing))

julia> Base.specializations(methods(g)[1])
Base.MethodSpecializations(svec(MethodInstance for g(::Int64, ::Int64, ::Int64), MethodInstance for g(::Int64, ::Int64, ::Float64),
 MethodInstance for g(::Int64, ::Int64, ::ComplexF64), nothing, nothing, nothing, nothing))

julia> Base.specializations(methods(h)[1])
Base.MethodSpecializations(MethodInstance for h(::Int64, ::Int64, ::Any))
```
In short, despite the throwaway parameter being unused, the functions specialize on it regardless, *even if `@nospecialize` is used*, unless the throwaway parameter is given a name.

This leads to the generation of a lot of useless code, potentially significantly increasing compile times. As a more realistic example, closer to how I ran across this issue in my code while trying to reduce precompilation times, consider the following code which defines an interface:

```julia
# Interface definition
abstract type Model end

function do_something(::T, _, _) where {T <: Model}
    throw(ArgumentError("$(nameof(T)) has not implemented the `do_something` interface!"))
end

# Model1 uses both parameters
struct Model1 <: Model end

function do_something(::Model1, param1, param2)
    return param1 + param2
end

# Model2 uses only one param
struct Model2 <: Model
    val::Float64
end

function do_something(m::Model2, param1, _)
    return param1 * m.val
end

# Model3 does not implement the interface
struct Model3 <: Model end

try
    do_something(Model3(), 1.0, 2.0)
catch
end

try
    do_something(Model3(), 1.0, 2)
catch
end

do_something(Model1(), 1.0, 2.0)
do_something(Model1(), 1.0, 2)
do_something(Model2(5.0), 1.0, 2.0)
do_something(Model2(5.0), 1.0, 2)

@show length(Base.specializations(methods(do_something)[1])) # 2
@show length(Base.specializations(methods(do_something)[2])) # 2
@show length(Base.specializations(methods(do_something)[2])) # 2
```

Ideally, neither the fallback method of `do_something` nor the one taking a `Model2` would specialize on their unused parameters. However, each generates two specializations. This can be fixed by using @no_specialize and a named parameter,
but is quite ugly and pretty advanced. It can also lead to superfluous IDE tooling warnings about unused parameters.

# Versions
@mcabbott tested this and found that it occurs on both v1.10 and nightly. Hi @archermarx I've just started working through the checklist. > > * I couldn't find guidelines for "Report issues or problems with the software" or "Seek support". This could be fixed with a couple of sentences on the "Home" page of your documentation, for example. > > * Link to tutorial from the README is broken > > * PR with very small update to install instruction here: [Update installation instructions UM-PEPL/HallThruster.jl#83](https://github.com/UM-PEPL/HallThruster.jl/pull/83) (tested on Julia 1.9) I've accepted your PR and made the relevant changes. > Tutorials: > > * I had to add `show=true` when using `plot!` within a user-defined function. For example, in the tutorial function `plot_current`: `plot!(p, t_ms, ion_current, label = "Ion current (cathode)") --> `plot!(p, t_ms, ion_current, label = "Ion current (cathode)",show=true)` (would also need updating at other places). > > > It may be that your system is setup to output plot as default. I am using a fresh install of Julia and IJulia. May catch others out so may be worth making a note of behaviour if don't want to add additional keyword argument to every `plot` call (which does feel clunky way to handle). > > * There are some warnings towards the end of the tutorial. Is this acceptable? May be worth making a note if so, or fix if not. Thanks for this. I added a note about the warnings to the tutorial and changed the plot parts of the tutorial to explicitly call `display`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/1597396880/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I updated my checklist. I could also download and run the software on my new personal laptop so I have ticked off a few boxes in the documentation and functionality section as well. In the documentation, > > 1. Few equations, such as that for electron heat conduction and two-zone Bohm collision frequency are not printing in math mode, possibly a missing bracket or something like that. > > 2. In the tutorial, it is unclear how an anomalous electron transport model is selected. > > > I could not find a statement of need and community guidelines in the documentation. If the authors could add that, or point me to where its located, I can tick off the corresponding boxes. Other than that, the documentation looks quite detailed and informative :) Thanks for the comments! Rupali! Thank you for your feedback. Let me try and address some of the concerns. Note that many of these were not included in the paper due to the word limit. I don't know if you had a chance to look through the documentation, but many of these ought to be covered there. > 1. Line 25: The electron flux causing the problem is actually parallel to the applied electric field. The 'Hall' current in the direction perpendicular to both magnetic and electric fields (i.e. in the azimuthal direction), but the anomalous electron transport is primarily in the axial direction. > Yes, this is a typo. It should say "perpendicular to the applied magnetic field" > 2. An explanation is needed on why the lesser-known flux schemes are used instead of the more standard schemes like HLLC, Steger-Warming, Roe's method, etc. I'm not sure these are necessarily lesser-known. Rusanov/Local Lax Friedrichs is a particularly well-known scheme in CFD, particularly within education, and the Global Lax Friedrichs is a straightforward modification of this scheme. I do agree it would be good to discuss why these fluxes were chosen, but due to wordcount limitations, I think most of this would have to be relegated to the documentation. Adding more fluxes is definitely something we'd like to do. > 4. Details for the fluid model used for comparing the results should be mentioned to some extent. It is unclear at this point if the fluid models are expected to be more or less accurate than the code developed in this study. > That would be good to include. The LANDMARK benchmark code should be somewhat less accurate than our code, but it difficult to compare in detail because no numerical information is provided with the benchmark. > 5. In general, a code/software presented must be evaluated for its accuracy and limitations. While the comparison results are presented, no discussion is made on the why the differences occur, and in what cases would this code not provide reliable results at all. For instance, removing the anomalous collision would bring the Knudsen number dangerously close to 1, and a fluid description is inaccurate. Also mention under what circumstances does a 1D approximation not hold for a hall thruster. > Agree. I think more discussion is warranted, but again I am unsure whether that belongs in the paper or in the documentation of the code. > 6. The list of references seems incomplete. Many researchers have worked on fluid/hybrid models of plasma for hall thrusters. It would be helpful to place the current code in context of the existing codes. Yes. I think adding more references would definitely be good. @lucydot do references count toward the word limit? {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/388","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/388/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/388/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/388/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/388","id":1319247306,"node_id":"I_kwDOCxDvo85Ooh3K","number":388,"title":"Hyperparameter optimization for kriging model","user":{"login":"chenr86","id":95456600,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbCNWA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95456600?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86","html_url":"https://github.com/chenr86","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-07-27T09:03:34Z","updated_at":"2022-08-01T17:49:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hello,

I am using surrogates.jl v6.3.0 to build kriging for a dataset with 500 elements. There are 24 input variables and 1 output. But I found the accuracy is terrible. There is serious overfitting with the model. 

```
using CSV 
using DataFrames
using Plots
using Surrogates
using SurrogatesPolyChaos
using StatsPlots
using Statistics
default()
using Dates
using LinearAlgebra
using Lathe.preprocess: TrainTestSplit

df = DataFrame(CSV.File("result.csv"))
train, test = TrainTestSplit(df, .8)
dim = 24

x_train = train[:, 1:dim]
x_train = values.(eachrow(x_train))
y_train = train[:, end]
# y_train = values.(eachrow(y_train))

x_test = test[:, 1:dim]
x_test = values.(eachrow(x_test))
y_test = test[:, end]

lower_bound = [
    200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 
    200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200,
    180, 180, 180, 180, 180, 180,
    300, 300, 300, 300, 300, 300
]
upper_bound = [
    230, 230, 230, 230, 230, 230,
    275, 275, 275, 275, 275, 275,
    200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200,
    350, 350, 350, 350, 350, 350
]
p = ones(dim) * 2
theta = [0.5 / max(1e-6 * norm(upper_bound .- lower_bound), std(x_i[i] for x_i in x_train))^p[i] for i in 1:length(x_train[1])]
mymodel = Kriging(x_train, y_train, lower_bound, upper_bound, p=p, theta=theta)

# Prediction
ys_test = mymodel.(x_test)
ys_train = mymodel.(x_train)

# Model assessment criteria
function mae(x, y)
    return sum(abs.(x - y)) / length(x)
end

function mape(x, y)
    return sum(abs.(x - y)/y) / length(x)
end

function rmse(x, y)
    return sqrt(sum(((x - y).^2) / length(x)))
end

function mse(x, y)
    return sum(((x - y).^2) / length(x))
end

function r2(x,y)
    sse = sum((x - y).^2)
    sst = sum((y .- mean(y)).^2)
    return 1 - sse / sst
end

println("      ASSESSMENT CRITERIA            TRAIN              TEST")
println("      Mean Absolute Error       ", mae(ys_train, y_train), "      ", mae(ys_test, y_test))
println("Mean Absolute Percentage Error  ", mape(ys_train, y_train), "      ", mape(ys_test, y_test))
println("    Root Mean Square Error      ", rmse(ys_train, y_train), "      ", rmse(ys_test, y_test))
println("       Mean Square Error        ", mse(ys_train, y_train), "      ", mse(ys_test, y_test))
println("           R Square             ", r2(ys_train, y_train), "      ", r2(ys_test, y_test))
```

According to https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/368, I calculate the loss function, `Inf` is obtained! 

```
function kriging_logpdf(params, x, y, lb, ub)
    d = length(params) ÷ 2
    theta = params[1:d]
    p = params[d+1:end]
    surr = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub; p, theta)

    n = length(y)
    y_minus_1μ = y - ones(length(y), 1) * surr.mu
    Rinv = surr.inverse_of_R

    term1 = only(-(y_minus_1μ' * surr.inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / 2 / surr.sigma)
    term2 = -log((2π * surr.sigma)^(n/2) / sqrt(det(Rinv)))

    logpdf = term1 + term2
    return logpdf
end

loss_func = params -> -kriging_logpdf(params, x_train, y_train, lower_bound, upper_bound)

loss_func([theta; p])
```

I don't know how can I improve my model, as p and theta are already the values suggested. mymodel = Kriging(x_train, y_train, lower_bound, upper_bound, p=p, theta=theta)

# Prediction
ys_test = mymodel.(x_test)
ys_train = mymodel.(x_train)

# Model assessment criteria
function mae(x, y)
    return sum(abs.(x - y)) / length(x)
end

function mape(x, y)
    return sum(abs.(x - y)/y) / length(x)
end

function rmse(x, y)
    return sqrt(sum(((x - y).^2) / length(x)))
end

function mse(x, y)
    return sum(((x - y).^2) / length(x))
end

function r2(x,y)
    sse = sum((x - y).^2)
    sst = sum((y .- mean(y)).^2)
    return 1 - sse / sst
end

println("      ASSESSMENT CRITERIA            TRAIN              TEST")

println("    Root Mean Square Error      ", rmse(ys_train, y_train), "      ", rmse(ys_test, y_test))
println("       Mean Square Error        ", mse(ys_train, y_train), "      ", mse(ys_test, y_test))
println("           R Square             ", r2(ys_train, y_train), "      ", r2(ys_test, y_test))
```

According to https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/368, I calculate the loss function, `Inf` is obtained! 

```
function kriging_logpdf(params, x, y, lb, ub)
    d = length(params) ÷ 2
    theta = params[1:d]
    p = params[d+1:end]
    surr = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub; p, theta)

    n = length(y)
    y_minus_1μ = y - ones(length(y), 1) * surr.mu
    Rinv = surr.inverse_of_R

    term1 = only(-(y_minus_1μ' * surr.inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / 2 / surr.sigma)
    term2 = -log((2π * surr.sigma)^(n/2) / sqrt(det(Rinv)))

    logpdf = term1 + term2
    return logpdf
end

loss_func = params -> -kriging_logpdf(params, x_train, y_train, lower_bound, upper_bound)

loss_func([theta; p])
```

I don't know how can I improve my model, as p and theta are already the values suggested. Kriging differentiable","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-07-07T19:55:02Z","updated_at":"2022-07-07T19:55:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In order to make hyperparameter optimization of gaussian process models simpler (cf https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/328 and https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/328), it would be nice to be able to use AD on kriging surrogates. This turns out not to be too difficult. Making only two changes in the specification of Kriging allows Zygote to compute gradients with respect to hyperparameters without a fuss:

Setup:
```julia
using Surrogates
using LinearAlgebra 

function branin(x)
    x1 = x[1]
    x2 = x[2]
    b = 5.1 / (4*pi^2);
    c = 5/pi;
    r = 6;
    a = 1;
    s = 10;
    t = 1 / (8*pi);
    term1 = a * (x2 - b*x1^2 + c*x1 - r)^2;
    term2 = s*(1-t)*cos(x1);
    y = term1 + term2 + s;
end

n_samples = 21
lower_bound = [-5.0, 0.0]
upper_bound = [10.0, 15.0]

xys = sample(n_samples, lower_bound, upper_bound, SobolSample())
zs = branin.(xys);

# good hyperparams for the Branin function
p = [2.0, 2.0]
theta = [0.03, 0.003]

kriging_surrogate = Kriging(xys, zs, lower_bound, upper_bound, p, theta)

# Log likelihood function of a Gaussian process model, AKA what we want to optimize in order to tune our hyperparameters
function kriging_logpdf(params, x, y, lb, ub)
    d = length(params) ÷ 2
    theta = params[1:d]
    p = params[d+1:end]
    surr = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub; p, theta)

    n = length(y)
    y_minus_1μ = y - ones(length(y), 1) * surr.mu
    Rinv = surr.inverse_of_R

    term1 = only(-(y_minus_1μ' * surr.inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / 2 / surr.sigma)
    term2 = -log((2π * surr.sigma)^(n/2) / sqrt(det(Rinv)))

    logpdf = term1 + term2
    return logpdf
end

loss_func = params -> -kriging_logpdf(params, xys, zs, lower_bound, upper_bound)
```

Just to check that this works, here's how the model performs


![plot_87](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/177857563-ff3e9749-1369-4ed8-a195-7598ea9da100.png)


Now let's try taking a gradient with respect to our hyperparameters $p, \theta$

```julia
using Zygote

Zygote.gradient(loss_func, [p; theta])
```

We get an error (`ERROR: Mutating arrays is not supported -- called setindex!(Matrix{Float64}, ...)`) which comes from the `_calc_kriging_coeffs` function when we build the covariance matrix `R`. We can replace the mutating part of this function with a matrix comprehension as follows:

old:

```julia

function _calc_kriging_coeffs(x, y, p, theta)
    n = length(x)
    d = length(x[1])
    R = zeros(float(eltype(x[1])), n, n)
    @inbounds for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            sum = zero(eltype(x[1]))
            for l in 1:d
                sum = sum + theta[l] * norm(x[i][l] - x[j][l])^p[l]
            end
            R[i, j] = exp(-sum)
        end
    end

    one = ones(n, 1)
    one_t = one'
    inverse_of_R = inv(R)

    mu = (one_t * inverse_of_R * y) / (one_t * inverse_of_R * one)

    y_minus_1μ = y - one * mu

    b = inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ

    sigma = (y_minus_1μ' * inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / n

    mu[1], b, sigma[1], inverse_of_R
end
```

new:

```julia

function _calc_kriging_coeffs(x, y, p, theta)
    n = length(x)
    d = length(x[1])
    R = [
        let
            sum = zero(eltype(x[1]))
            for l in 1:d
                sum = sum + theta[l] * norm(x[i][l] - x[j][l])^p[l]
            end
            exp(-sum)
        end

        for j in 1:n, i in 1:n
    ]

    one = ones(n, 1)
    one_t = one'
    inverse_of_R = inv(R)

    mu = (one_t * inverse_of_R * y) / (one_t * inverse_of_R * one)

    y_minus_1μ = y - one * mu

    b = inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ

    sigma = (y_minus_1μ' * inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / n

    mu[1], b, sigma[1], inverse_of_R
end
```

With that, we can compute a gradient!

```julia
julia> Zygote.gradient(loss_func, [0.03, 0.0034, 2.0, 2.0])
([-654.8265400112359, 1784.234854500508, 5532.336307947245, 332.2643207182241],)
```

Kriging differentiable","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-07-07T19:55:02Z","updated_at":"2022-07-07T19:56:40Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In order to make hyperparameter optimization of gaussian process models simpler (cf https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/328 and https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/328), it would be nice to be able to use AD on kriging surrogates. This turns out not to be too difficult. Making only two changes in the specification of Kriging allows Zygote to compute gradients with respect to hyperparameters without a fuss:

Setup:
```julia
using Surrogates
using LinearAlgebra 

function branin(x)
    x1 = x[1]
    x2 = x[2]
    b = 5.1 / (4*pi^2);
    c = 5/pi;
    r = 6;
    a = 1;
    s = 10;
    t = 1 / (8*pi);
    term1 = a * (x2 - b*x1^2 + c*x1 - r)^2;
    term2 = s*(1-t)*cos(x1);
    y = term1 + term2 + s;
end

n_samples = 21
lower_bound = [-5.0, 0.0]
upper_bound = [10.0, 15.0]

xys = sample(n_samples, lower_bound, upper_bound, SobolSample())
zs = branin.(xys);

# good hyperparams for the Branin function
p = [2.0, 2.0]
theta = [0.03, 0.003]

kriging_surrogate = Kriging(xys, zs, lower_bound, upper_bound, p, theta)

# Log likelihood function of a Gaussian process model, AKA what we want to optimize in order to tune our hyperparameters
function kriging_logpdf(params, x, y, lb, ub)
    d = length(params) ÷ 2
    theta = params[1:d]
    p = params[d+1:end]
    surr = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub; p, theta)

    n = length(y)
    y_minus_1μ = y - ones(length(y), 1) * surr.mu
    Rinv = surr.inverse_of_R

    term1 = only(-(y_minus_1μ' * surr.inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / 2 / surr.sigma)
    term2 = -log((2π * surr.sigma)^(n/2) / sqrt(det(Rinv)))

    logpdf = term1 + term2
    return logpdf
end

loss_func = params -> -kriging_logpdf(params, xys, zs, lower_bound, upper_bound)
```

Just to check that this works, here's how the model performs


![plot_87](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/177857563-ff3e9749-1369-4ed8-a195-7598ea9da100.png)


Now let's try taking a gradient with respect to our hyperparameters $p, \theta$

```julia
using Zygote

Zygote.gradient(loss_func, [p; theta])
```

We get an error (`ERROR: Mutating arrays is not supported -- called setindex!(Matrix{Float64}, ...)`) which comes from the `_calc_kriging_coeffs` function when we build the covariance matrix `R`. We can replace the mutating part of this function with a matrix comprehension as follows:

old:

```julia

function _calc_kriging_coeffs(x, y, p, theta)
    n = length(x)
    d = length(x[1])
    R = zeros(float(eltype(x[1])), n, n)
    @inbounds for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            sum = zero(eltype(x[1]))
            for l in 1:d
                sum = sum + theta[l] * norm(x[i][l] - x[j][l])^p[l]
            end
            R[i, j] = exp(-sum)
        end
    end

    one = ones(n, 1)
    one_t = one'
    inverse_of_R = inv(R)

    mu = (one_t * inverse_of_R * y) / (one_t * inverse_of_R * one)

    y_minus_1μ = y - one * mu

    b = inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ

    sigma = (y_minus_1μ' * inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / n

    mu[1], b, sigma[1], inverse_of_R
end
```

new:

```julia

function _calc_kriging_coeffs(x, y, p, theta)
    n = length(x)
    d = length(x[1])
    R = [
        let
            sum = zero(eltype(x[1]))
            for l in 1:d
                sum = sum + theta[l] * norm(x[i][l] - x[j][l])^p[l]
            end
            exp(-sum)
        end

        for j in 1:n, i in 1:n
    ]

    one = ones(n, 1)
    one_t = one'
    inverse_of_R = inv(R)

    mu = (one_t * inverse_of_R * y) / (one_t * inverse_of_R * one)

    y_minus_1μ = y - one * mu

    b = inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ

    sigma = (y_minus_1μ' * inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / n

    mu[1], b, sigma[1], inverse_of_R
end
```

With that, we can compute a gradient!

```julia
julia> Zygote.gradient(loss_func, [0.03, 0.0034, 2.0, 2.0])
([-654.8265400112359, 1784.234854500508, 5532.336307947245, 332.2643207182241],)
```

Kriging differentiable and better initial hyperparams","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-07-07T19:55:02Z","updated_at":"2022-07-08T20:22:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In order to make hyperparameter optimization of gaussian process models simpler (cf https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/328 and https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/328), it would be nice to be able to use AD on kriging surrogates. This turns out not to be too difficult. Making only a single change to the computation of Kriging coefficients makes these surrogates differentiable with respect to their hyperparameters

Setup:
```julia
using Surrogates
using LinearAlgebra 

function branin(x)
    x1 = x[1]
    x2 = x[2]
    b = 5.1 / (4*pi^2);
    c = 5/pi;
    r = 6;
    a = 1;
    s = 10;
    t = 1 / (8*pi);
    term1 = a * (x2 - b*x1^2 + c*x1 - r)^2;
    term2 = s*(1-t)*cos(x1);
    y = term1 + term2 + s;
end

n_samples = 21
lower_bound = [-5.0, 0.0]
upper_bound = [10.0, 15.0]

xys = sample(n_samples, lower_bound, upper_bound, SobolSample())
zs = branin.(xys);

# good hyperparams for the Branin function
p = [2.0, 2.0]
theta = [0.03, 0.003]

kriging_surrogate = Kriging(xys, zs, lower_bound, upper_bound, p, theta)

# Log likelihood function of a Gaussian process model, AKA what we want to optimize in order to tune our hyperparameters
function kriging_logpdf(params, x, y, lb, ub)
    d = length(params) ÷ 2
    theta = params[1:d]
    p = params[d+1:end]
    surr = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub; p, theta)

    n = length(y)
    y_minus_1μ = y - ones(length(y), 1) * surr.mu
    Rinv = surr.inverse_of_R

    term1 = only(-(y_minus_1μ' * surr.inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / 2 / surr.sigma)
    term2 = -log((2π * surr.sigma)^(n/2) / sqrt(det(Rinv)))

    logpdf = term1 + term2
    return logpdf
end

loss_func = params -> -kriging_logpdf(params, xys, zs, lower_bound, upper_bound)
```

Just to check that this works, here's how the model performs


![plot_87](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/177857563-ff3e9749-1369-4ed8-a195-7598ea9da100.png)


Now let's try taking a gradient with respect to our hyperparameters $p, \theta$

```julia
using Zygote

Zygote.gradient(loss_func, [p; theta])
```

We get an error (`ERROR: Mutating arrays is not supported -- called setindex!(Matrix{Float64}, ...)`) which comes from the `_calc_kriging_coeffs` function when we build the covariance matrix `R`. We can replace the mutating part of this function with a matrix comprehension as follows:

old:

```julia

function _calc_kriging_coeffs(x, y, p, theta)
    n = length(x)
    d = length(x[1])
    R = zeros(float(eltype(x[1])), n, n)
    @inbounds for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            sum = zero(eltype(x[1]))
            for l in 1:d
                sum = sum + theta[l] * norm(x[i][l] - x[j][l])^p[l]
            end
            R[i, j] = exp(-sum)
        end
    end

    one = ones(n, 1)
    one_t = one'
    inverse_of_R = inv(R)

    mu = (one_t * inverse_of_R * y) / (one_t * inverse_of_R * one)

    y_minus_1μ = y - one * mu

    b = inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ

    sigma = (y_minus_1μ' * inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / n

    mu[1], b, sigma[1], inverse_of_R
end
```

new:

```julia

function _calc_kriging_coeffs(x, y, p, theta)
    n = length(x)
    d = length(x[1])
    R = [
        let
            sum = zero(eltype(x[1]))
            for l in 1:d
                sum = sum + theta[l] * norm(x[i][l] - x[j][l])^p[l]
            end
            exp(-sum)
        end

        for j in 1:n, i in 1:n
    ]

    one = ones(n, 1)
    one_t = one'
    inverse_of_R = inv(R)

    mu = (one_t * inverse_of_R * y) / (one_t * inverse_of_R * one)

    y_minus_1μ = y - one * mu

    b = inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ

    sigma = (y_minus_1μ' * inverse_of_R * y_minus_1μ) / n

    mu[1], b, sigma[1], inverse_of_R
end
```

With that, we can compute a gradient!

```julia
julia> Zygote.gradient(loss_func, [0.03, 0.0034, 2.0, 2.0])
([-654.8265400112359, 1784.234854500508, 5532.336307947245, 332.2643207182241],)
```

For the initial hyperparams, I propose:

$$p_i = 2, i \in [1, d]$$

$$\theta_i = \frac{1}{2}\frac{1}{\mathrm{variance}(\mathbf{X_i})}, i\in [1, d]$$

The latter comes from [A Practical Guide to Gaussian Processes](https://infallible-thompson-49de36.netlify.app/) and the interpretation of $\theta_i = 1/2l_i^2$, where $l_i$ is a characteristic correlation length scale for variable $i$. Scaling the length scale with the standard deviation of the samples makes the hyperparameter initialization independent of the box size, which is a very desirable property. With this initialization, here's some comparisons to the default initialization:

## Sphere function
**Old**

![l1norm_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064781-213b1545-52c9-4854-8f0c-37dfb95621bf.png)

## Rosenbrock

**Old**

Clearly, the new hyperparameters are a significant improvement.

I'll make a PR with this, but we'd also want to update all of the documentation examples using Kriging to use the new default hyperparameters as the current examples don't exactly give the most confidence in the method's performance.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/comments/1179333936/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

beyond 3 dimensions and Kriging seems to produce nearly the same results for all inputs. 

To reproduce the issue please try the following code: 

```
using Surrogates

function sphere_function(x)
    return sum(x .^ 2)
end

n = 20
ndim = 4
lb = [-5.0 for i in 1:ndim]
ub = [5.0 for i in 1:ndim]
x = sample(n, lb, ub, SobolSample())
n_test = 5
x_test = sample(n_test, lb, ub, GoldenSample())
my_y = sphere_function.(x)
my_krig = Kriging(x, my_y, lb, ub)
y_krig = my_krig.(x_test)
```

See https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/368 for some details, but here's what the sphere function looks like in 2D using the default parameters:

![sphere_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178065464-eb5dc5ca-0606-45c0-adda-8e329b20b38c.png)


You can see that the kriging model does a terrible job of interpolating between the data. As the data gets even sparser in higher dimensions, this problem gets worse and worse. If you're even a small distance away from one of the points, the kriging model will just return the estimated mean of all of the data. With my proposed better default hyperparameters, the surrogate looks much more reasonable:

![sphere_new_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178065593-821abfeb-c3b2-41b3-aa1d-bf068b458263.png)

beyond 3 dimensions and Kriging seems to produce nearly the same results for all inputs. 

To reproduce the issue please try the following code: 

```
using Surrogates

function sphere_function(x)
    return sum(x .^ 2)
end

n = 20
ndim = 4
lb = [-5.0 for i in 1:ndim]
ub = [5.0 for i in 1:ndim]
x = sample(n, lb, ub, SobolSample())
n_test = 5
x_test = sample(n_test, lb, ub, GoldenSample())
my_y = sphere_function.(x)
my_krig = Kriging(x, my_y, lb, ub)
y_krig = my_krig.(x_test)
```



","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/369/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/369/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/comments/1179345427","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/369#issuecomment-1179345427","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/369","id":1179345427,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCxDvo85GS2IT","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-07-08T20:41:36Z","updated_at":"2022-07-08T20:41:36Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"To go a bit further, here's what we get running your initial problem:

```julia
n = 20
ndim = 4
lb = [-5.0 for i in 1:ndim]
ub = [5.0 for i in 1:ndim]
x = sample(n, lb, ub, SobolSample())
n_test = 5
x_test = sample(n_test, lb, ub, GoldenSample())
my_y = sphere_function.(x)
my_krig = Kriging(x, my_y, lb, ub)
```
```julia
julia> y_test = sphere_function.(x_test)
5-element Vector{Float64}:
 36.92498923651821
 55.98457436217706
 32.29817769991404
 19.31107589765888
 27.125065405299406

julia> y_krig = my_krig.(x_test)
5-element Vector{Float64}:
 33.37346160876789
 33.44511179109669
 33.75970362487952
 33.428604779202786
 33.464603788248986

julia> my_krig.mu
33.42697078971461
```
All of the returned values are very close to `my_krig.mu`, which is the mean of the Gaussian process. If we initialize our hyperparameters more intelligently:

```julia
p = fill(2.0, ndim)
theta = [
    let
        0.5 / var(x_i[1] for x_i in x)
    end

    for i in 1:ndim
]

my_krig = Kriging(x, my_y, lb, ub; p, theta)
```
```julia
julia> y_krig = my_krig.(x_test)
5-element Vector{Float64}:
 29.66978970250584
 37.65818821603272
 46.72706086763887
 32.41137510932391
 32.39225405187181
```

We no longer get nearly identical results. The values are better than before, but not great. 20 points isn't much for four dimensions, so following a "(10 times the number of dimensions) + 1" rule, we can get even better results:

```julia

ndim = 4
n = 10 * ndim + 1
lb = [-5.0 for i in 1:ndim]
ub = [5.0 for i in 1:ndim]
x = sample(n, lb, ub, SobolSample())

p = fill(2.0, ndim)
theta = [
    let
        0.5 / var(x_i[1] for x_i in x)
    end

    for i in 1:ndim
]

n_test = 5
x_test = sample(n_test, lb, ub, GoldenSample())
my_y = sphere_function.(x)

my_krig = Kriging(x, my_y, lb, ub; p, theta)
```
```julia
julia> y_test = sphere_function.(x_test)
5-element Vector{Float64}:
 36.92498923651821
 55.98457436217706
 32.29817769991404
 19.31107589765888
 27.125065405299406

julia> y_krig = my_krig.(x_test)
5-element Vector{Float64}:
 31.76694949520314
 43.06491221349952
 39.24082303641144
 20.951443558751766
 30.49673569023502

These are significantly better, and would be further improved by proper hyperparameter optimization.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/comments/1179345427/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":374,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pulls/374","id":993551411,"node_id":"PR_kwDOCxDvo847OGQz","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/374","diff_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/374.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/374.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/374","number":374,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"New default hyperparameters and miscellaneous bug fixes for Kriging.","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Addresses https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/369 and https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/368. This PR defines new default hyperparameters for the `Kriging` surrogate. Along the way, I found that the implementation of the 1D kriging surrogate was incorrect and did not work properly for theta not equal to 1, so I've fixed that as well. 

To summarize, the default hyperparameters for `Kriging` used to be `p = ones(d)`, `theta = ones(d)`, where `d` is the problem dimension. These are very bad choices. I have changed them to

$$p_i = 2, i \in [1, d]$$

$$\theta_i = \frac{1}{2}\frac{1}{\mathrm{variance}(\mathbf{X_i})}, i\in [1, d]$$

The latter comes from [A Practical Guide to Gaussian Processes](https://infallible-thompson-49de36.netlify.app/) and the interpretation of $\theta_i = 1/2l_i^2$, where $l_i$ is a characteristic correlation length scale for variable $i$. Scaling the length scale with the standard deviation of the samples makes the hyperparameter initialization independent of the box size, which is a very desirable property. With this initialization, here's some comparisons to the old defaults:

## Sphere function
**Old**
![sphere_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064647-aa30a437-1e36-40b1-91ce-02ce6f5aa874.png)
**New**
![sphere_new_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064646-410e519b-8d72-4dce-ba44-c6cd01a43964.png)

## L1-norm function
**Old**
![l1norm_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064781-213b1545-52c9-4854-8f0c-37dfb95621bf.png)
**New**
![l1norm_new_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064776-b93f6506-ae4d-4e25-a109-1e5ca5958c56.png)

## Branin function
**Old**
![branin_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064869-4bc3ba1e-a2ea-4491-8327-5847f8e44e40.png)

**New**
![branin_new_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064891-f45ab9f8-8868-42d8-947c-57d5954e4d36.png)

## Rosenbrock

**Old**
![rosenbrock_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064945-68eb2f3c-36ba-49f6-bc49-ad606b46ca35.png)

**New**
![rosenbrock_new_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064965-612a457e-87b5-4b33-9a7f-59df5da14229.png)


Clearly, the new hyperparameters are a significant improvement. During implementation, I found that the 1D kriging surrogate only worked properly when theta = 1, so I have fixed that as well:

**Old (with old default hyperparams)**
![plot_169](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178356584-3d663d9c-3a5a-44ab-a25b-1812fc48af21.png)

**Old (with new default hyperparams)**
![plot_171](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178356965-04288a7f-2678-4c44-98b1-a704a7d45032.png)

**New (with new default hyperparams)**
![plot_170](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178356601-2d74e437-1640-428d-9223-51c5df84a529.png)

Still to do is update the documentation for kriging to make use of the better defaults, as the current documentation doesn't make kriging look all that {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/251","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/251/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/251/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/251/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/251","id":822216713,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU4MjIyMTY3MTM=","number":251,"title":"Performance of kriging compared to mogp-emulator?","user":{"login":"sdwfrost","id":2164195,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxNjQxOTU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2164195?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost","html_url":"https://github.com/sdwfrost","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sdwfrost/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2021-03-04T15:03:47Z","updated_at":"2022-07-11T21:20:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm trying to implement history matching; the basic idea is quite simple - you make an emulator that can generate standard errors (e.g. a Gaussian process) and train on quite a small number of simulations. Then, for a given datapoint (or datapoints), one calculates a measure of implausibility (e.g. mean/se), and excludes datapoints above a threshold (eg 3).

As an example, I'm following the [tutorial for the Python library mogp-emulator](https://mogp-emulator.readthedocs.io/en/latest/intro/tutorial.html). I have written a Jupyter notebook so you can see the output, available [as a gist here](https://gist.github.com/sdwfrost/238d86efad830266d707c9437b637cc0).

My port of this example to `Surrogates.jl` is [here](https://gist.github.com/sdwfrost/921658ad32df4370e0426cc64231060d) using `Kriging`. Note that my calculation of implausibility isn't as general as the one in `mogp-emulator`.

The main problem is that while I get good reproduction of the output for both mogp-emulator and Surrogates.jl for the training set (n=50), I can't get good performance on a validation set (n=10) for Surrogates. The performance of `mogp-emulator` varies stochastically depending on the training samples, but after a run or two, it isn't hard to get fits for the validation set that are good enough to rule out a large part of parameter space using implausibility. In contrast, I can't seem to get the same performance out of `Surrogates.jl`. I haven't gone into the innards of the code for both libraries yet, but any pointers as to where the implementations are diverging would be great.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/251/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/251/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/comments/1180881833","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/251#issuecomment-1180881833","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/251","id":1180881833,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCxDvo85GYtOp","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-07-11T21:20:28Z","updated_at":"2022-07-11T21:20:28Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This looks like another case of bad hyperparameters. You have `p = [1.0, 5.0]` which are not going to give great results, `p = 5.0` is out of bounds. With the new default hyperparameters proposed in https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/374, we get this:

![plot_188](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178360355-9363797e-2124-4be1-a196-8b59fa13583a.png)

This was accomplished by changing one line in the gist

**Old**
```julia
gp = Kriging(simulation_points, simulation_output, lower_bound, upper_bound, p = [1.0, 5.0]);
```
to 

**New**
```julia
gp = Kriging(simulation_points, simulation_output, lower_bound, upper_bound);
```

compare this to the python example you provide (for which hyperparameters were trained/fit)

![Screenshot 2022-07-11 171656](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178359925-1c9784d7-4d07-4d60-9736-abea7a3774b8.png)

https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/369 and https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/368. This PR defines new default hyperparameters for the `Kriging` surrogate. Along the way, I found that the implementation of the 1D kriging surrogate was incorrect and did not work properly for theta not equal to 1, so I've fixed that as well. 

To summarize, the default hyperparameters for `Kriging` used to be `p = ones(d)`, `theta = ones(d)`, where `d` is the problem dimension. These are very bad choices. I have changed them to

$$p_i = 2, i \in [1, d]$$

$$\theta_i = \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{std}(\mathbf{X_i})^{-p_i}, i\in [1, d]$$

The latter comes from [A Practical Guide to Gaussian Processes](https://infallible-thompson-49de36.netlify.app/) and the interpretation of $\theta_i = 1/2l_i^2$, where $l_i$ is a characteristic correlation length scale for variable $i$. Scaling the length scale with the standard deviation of the samples makes the hyperparameter initialization independent of the box size, which is a very desirable property. With this initialization, here's some comparisons to the old defaults:

## Sphere function
**Old**
![sphere_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064647-aa30a437-1e36-40b1-91ce-02ce6f5aa874.png)
**New**
![sphere_new_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064646-410e519b-8d72-4dce-ba44-c6cd01a43964.png)

## L1-norm function
**Old**
![l1norm_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064781-213b1545-52c9-4854-8f0c-37dfb95621bf.png)
**New**
![l1norm_new_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064776-b93f6506-ae4d-4e25-a109-1e5ca5958c56.png)

## Branin function
**Old**
![branin_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064869-4bc3ba1e-a2ea-4491-8327-5847f8e44e40.png)

**New**
![branin_new_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064891-f45ab9f8-8868-42d8-947c-57d5954e4d36.png)

## Rosenbrock

**Old**
![rosenbrock_old_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064945-68eb2f3c-36ba-49f6-bc49-ad606b46ca35.png)

**New**
![rosenbrock_new_defaults](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178064965-612a457e-87b5-4b33-9a7f-59df5da14229.png)


Clearly, the new hyperparameters are a significant improvement. During implementation, I found that the 1D kriging surrogate only worked properly when theta = 1, so I have fixed that as well:

**Old (with old default hyperparams)**
![plot_169](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178356584-3d663d9c-3a5a-44ab-a25b-1812fc48af21.png)

**Old (with new default hyperparams)**
![plot_171](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178356965-04288a7f-2678-4c44-98b1-a704a7d45032.png)

**New (with new default hyperparams)**
![plot_170](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178356601-2d74e437-1640-428d-9223-51c5df84a529.png)

Still to do is update the documentation for kriging to make use of the better defaults, as the current documentation doesn't make kriging look all that effective","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/374/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/374/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/comments/1180915047","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/374#issuecomment-1180915047","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/374","id":1180915047,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCxDvo85GY1Vn","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-07-11T22:05:18Z","updated_at":"2022-07-11T22:05:18Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I have added nugget regularization to address the problem where the correlation matrix becomes singular when points are sufficiently close together. The way it currently works is to add a small "nugget" value to the main diagonal of the correlation matrix to keep the condition number less than 1e-8. This has the same effect as a tiny noise variance, and prevents the matrix from becoming singular at the cost of slightly relaxing the interpolation condition.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/comments/1180915047/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375","id":1302172625,"node_id":"I_kwDOCxDvo85NnZPR","number":375,"title":"Further Kriging improvements - Noise variance, log likelihood, and simplifying implementation","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-07-12T14:48:23Z","updated_at":"2022-07-12T14:48:23Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'd like to make a couple more changes to kriging

### 1. Noise variance hyperparameter

This would allow modeling and optimization of noisy functions. I would probably add this as a field to the `Kriging` struct. Noise variance is usually easier to estimate by the user than other hyperparameters, and would default to zero (aka the surrogate interpolates the data). I would also add an example to the documentation for minimizing a noisy function. 

One question: does adding fields to a struct count as a breaking change?

### 2. Log likelihood function

As another incremental step toward better hyperparameter optimization, I'd like to bundle the Kriging log likelihood function with Surrogates. I could then add a tutorial to the kriging section of the documentation on hyperparameter optimization.

### 3. Combine 1D and ND functions

I'm not sure of the historical reasons for this, but maintaining separate versions of the surrogate construction functions for 1D and ND data seems ripe for problems, as I found during https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/374. I have already tested that the ND versions work just fine on 1D data, but run slightly slower. It would take some thought to do it in a way that doesn't hurt performance, but it would allow us to remove a lot of code and make maintenance much easier.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375","id":1302172625,"node_id":"I_kwDOCxDvo85NnZPR","number":375,"title":"Further Kriging improvements - Noise variance, log likelihood, and simplifying implementation","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-07-12T14:48:23Z","updated_at":"2022-07-12T15:20:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'd like to make a couple more changes to kriging

### 1. Noise variance hyperparameter

This would allow modeling and optimization of noisy functions. I would probably add this as a field to the `Kriging` struct. Noise variance is usually easier to estimate by the user than other hyperparameters, and would default to zero (aka the surrogate interpolates the data). I would also add an example to the documentation for minimizing a noisy function. 

One question: does adding fields to a struct count as a breaking change?

### 2. Log likelihood function

As another incremental step toward better hyperparameter optimization, I'd like to bundle the Kriging log likelihood function with Surrogates. I could then add a tutorial to the kriging section of the documentation on hyperparameter optimization.

### 3. Combine 1D and ND functions

I'm not sure of the historical reasons for this, but maintaining separate versions of the surrogate construction functions for 1D and ND data seems ripe for problems, as I found during https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/374. I have already tested that the ND versions work just fine on 1D data, but run slightly slower. It would take some thought to do it in a way that doesn't hurt performance, but it would allow us to remove a lot of code and make maintenance much easier.

```julia
Random.seed!(1234)
    lb = 0.0
    ub = 10.0
    func = x -> sin(x)
    n_samples = 50
    x = sample(n_samples, lb, ub, SobolSample())

    # Add some random noise to the function
    y = func.(x) .+ 0.1 * randn(n_samples)

    # Build a kriging surrogate without noise
    my_k = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub, noise_variance = 0.0)

    x_fine = LinRange(lb, ub, 10000)
    y_fine = func.(x_fine)
    pred_fine = my_k.(x_fine)
    errs = std_error_at_point.(my_k, x_fine)
    p = Plots.plot(x_fine, y_fine, label = "True function", legend = :outertop)
    Plots.plot!(x_fine, pred_fine, label = "surrogate", ribbon= 3 * errs, ylims = (-2, 2))
    Plots.scatter!(my_k.x, my_k.y, mc = :black, label = "Data")
```
![plot_258](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178525448-a263e4be-3e64-4235-a4c2-fa2bcee6b2c0.png)

This interpolates the data but doesn't tell us much about how the signal varies

If we now instead include a noise variance, we get a much more accurate picture of the underlying function

```julia
    my_k = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub, noise_variance = 0.1)
```

![plot_257](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178525783-59cd9c5d-54a7-4b41-a805-f96e501705e5.png)

https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/337. Adds a new utility function `_check_input_dimension` which is called for all surrogates before evaluating a point. Provides a helpful error message if there's a dimension mismatch. 

# Example

```julia
using Surrogates

lb = 0.0
ub = 4.0
x = [1.0,2.0,3.0]
y = [4.0,5.0,6.0]
my_rad = RadialBasis(x, y, lb, ub, rad = linearRadial())
```

```julia
julia> my_rad(Float64[1.5])
4.5

julia> my_rad([1.0, 2.0])
ERROR: ArgumentError: This surrogate expects 1-dimensional inputs, but the input had dimension 2.
Stacktrace:
 [1] _check_dimension
   @ C:\Users\thoma\Surrogates.jl\src\utils.jl:11 [inlined]
 [2] (::RadialBasis{Surrogates.var"#1#2", Int64, Vector{Float64}, Vector{Float64}, Float64, Float64, Transpose{Float64, Vector{Float64}}, Float64, Bool})(val::Vector{Float64})
   @ Surrogates C:\Users\thoma\Surrogates.jl\src\Radials.jl:159
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[39]:1

julia> my_rad(Float64[])
ERROR: ArgumentError: This surrogate expects 1-dimensional inputs, but the input had dimension 0.
Stacktrace:
 [1] _check_dimension
   @ C:\Users\thoma\Surrogates.jl\src\utils.jl:11 [inlined]
 [2] (::RadialBasis{Surrogates.var"#1#2", Int64, Vector{Float64}, Vector{Float64}, Float64, Float64, Transpose{Float64, Vector{Float64}}, Float64, Bool})(val::Vector{Float64})
   @ Surrogates C:\Users\thoma\Surrogates.jl\src\Radials.jl:159
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[40]:1
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/378","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/378/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/378/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/378/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/378","id":1303125659,"node_id":"I_kwDOCxDvo85NrB6b","number":378,"title":" Degree modification of multivariate polynomial chaos surrogate model ","user":{"login":"chenr86","id":95456600,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbCNWA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95456600?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86","html_url":"https://github.com/chenr86","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/chenr86/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-07-13T08:59:12Z","updated_at":"2022-07-13T15:50:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hello,

I am using `Surrogate.jl` to build my polynomial choas surrogate model. The model is multivariate, with 4 input parameters, and 1D output. I want to change the parameter `op = SurrogatesPolyChaos.GaussOrthoPoly(5)` of the model, but I got an error message. What's the suitable form for multivariate polynomial chaos model? Can I specify the degree of the polynomial basis greater than 2 for multivariate model?

Below is an example and the error message.

```
using Surrogates
using SurrogatesPolyChaos
using Plots
default()
function f(x)
    t = x[1]
    w = x[2]
    L = 100.0
    E = 2.770674127819261e7
    X = 530.8038576066307
    Y = 997.8714938733949
    return (4*L^3)/(E*w*t)*sqrt( (Y/t^2)^2 + (X/w^2)^2)
end

n = 100
lb = [1.0,1.0]
ub = [8.0,8.0]
xys = sample(n,lb,ub,SobolSample());
zs = f.(xys);
x, y = 0:8, 0:8
p1 = surface(x, y, (x1,x2) -> f((x1,x2)))
xs = [xy[1] for xy in xys]
ys = [xy[2] for xy in xys]
p2 = contour(x, y, (x1,x2) -> f((x1,x2)))
scatter!(xs, ys)
plot(p1, p2, title="True function")

mypoly = PolynomialChaosSurrogate(xys, zs,  lb, ub, op = SurrogatesPolyChaos.GaussOrthoPoly(5))
```

{"action":"opened","number":379,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pulls/379","id":997072340,"node_id":"PR_kwDOCxDvo847bh3U","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/379","diff_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/379.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/379.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/379","number":379,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"More Kriging improvements (log likelihood, noise variance, more tests)","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This addresses https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375 as well as adds some more testing utilities to move us toward more rigorous and comprehensive property-based testing of surrogates.

## 1. Noise variance

The `Kriging` object now has a new field, `noise_variance`, which defaults to zero. This is a number added to the main diagonal of the covariance matrix which allows Kriging to model noisy functions

Here's what including noise variance looks like:

```julia
Random.seed!(1234)
  lb = 0.0
  ub = 10.0
  func = x -> sin(x)
  n_samples = 50
  x = sample(n_samples, lb, ub, SobolSample())

  # Add some random noise to the function
  y = func.(x) .+ 0.1 * randn(n_samples)

  # Build a kriging surrogate without noise
  my_k = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub, noise_variance = 0.0)

  x_fine = LinRange(lb, ub, 10000)
  y_fine = func.(x_fine)
  pred_fine = my_k.(x_fine)
  errs = std_error_at_point.(my_k, x_fine)
  p = Plots.plot(x_fine, y_fine, label = "True function", legend = :outertop)
  Plots.plot!(x_fine, pred_fine, label = "surrogate", ribbon= 3 * errs, ylims = (-2, 2))
  Plots.scatter!(my_k.x, my_k.y, mc = :black, label = "Data")
```
![plot_258](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178525448-a263e4be-3e64-4235-a4c2-fa2bcee6b2c0.png)

This interpolates the data but doesn't tell us much about how the signal varies

If we now instead include a noise variance, we get a much more accurate picture of the underlying function

```julia
my_k = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub, noise_variance = 0.1)
```

![plot_257](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178525783-59cd9c5d-54a7-4b41-a805-f96e501705e5.png)


## 2. Log likelihood function for kriging

There is now a `Surrogates.kriging_log_likelihood(x, y, p, theta, noise_variance)` function which is differentiable and can be used for hyperparameter optimization. 

## 3. Property-based testing

I have created a new file, `test_utils.jl` with two functions: `_random_surrogate` and `_check_interpolation`. The first generates a random surrogate (random dimension, number of points) of the given type and optionally using a provided function. The second checks that the input surrogate correctly interpolates its input data. Interpolation is an important property to check for most surrogates, and in cases when the surrogate regresses rather than interpolates (kriging with noise_variance not equal to zero, `linearSurrogate`, `SecondOrderPolynomialSurrogate`), we can check that that behavior also holds. I have added interpolation-checking tests to all surrogates except for Wendland, GEK, GEKPLS, and Earth. In testing, I found that Wendland doesn't seem to work at all, so that should be fixed.

## 4. Miscellaneous

I changed Kriging's default `p` from 2.0 to 1.99 to help numerical stability

## Still to do

- [ ] Add examples for kriging hyperparameter optimization to docs
- [ ] Add example for kriging noise variance to docs
- [ ] Fix Wendland surrogate
- [ ] Many doc examples are broken, would be good to fix tests)","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-07-14T20:03:48Z","updated_at":"2022-07-21T17:02:19Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pulls/379","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/379","diff_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/379.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/379.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This addresses https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/375 as well as adds some more testing utilities to move us toward more rigorous and comprehensive property-based testing of surrogates.

## 1. Noise variance

The `Kriging` object now has a new field, `noise_variance`, which defaults to zero. This is a number added to the main diagonal of the covariance matrix which allows Kriging to model noisy functions

Here's what including noise variance looks like:

```julia
Random.seed!(1234)
  lb = 0.0
  ub = 10.0
  func = x -> sin(x)
  n_samples = 50
  x = sample(n_samples, lb, ub, SobolSample())

  # Add some random noise to the function
  y = func.(x) .+ 0.1 * randn(n_samples)

  # Build a kriging surrogate without noise
  my_k = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub, noise_variance = 0.0)

  x_fine = LinRange(lb, ub, 10000)
  y_fine = func.(x_fine)
  pred_fine = my_k.(x_fine)
  errs = std_error_at_point.(my_k, x_fine)
  p = Plots.plot(x_fine, y_fine, label = "True function", legend = :outertop)
  Plots.plot!(x_fine, pred_fine, label = "surrogate", ribbon= 3 * errs, ylims = (-2, 2))
  Plots.scatter!(my_k.x, my_k.y, mc = :black, label = "Data")
```
![plot_258](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178525448-a263e4be-3e64-4235-a4c2-fa2bcee6b2c0.png)

This interpolates the data but doesn't tell us much about how the signal varies

If we now instead include a noise variance, we get a much more accurate picture of the underlying function

```julia
my_k = Kriging(x, y, lb, ub, noise_variance = 0.1)
```

![plot_257](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/178525783-59cd9c5d-54a7-4b41-a805-f96e501705e5.png)


## 2. Log likelihood function for kriging

There is now a `Surrogates.kriging_log_likelihood(x, y, p, theta, noise_variance)` function which is differentiable and can be used for hyperparameter optimization. 

## 3. Property-based testing

I have created a new file, `test_utils.jl` with two functions: `_random_surrogate` and `_check_interpolation`. The first generates a random surrogate (random dimension, number of points) of the given type and optionally using a provided function. The second checks that the input surrogate correctly interpolates its input data. Interpolation is an important property to check for most surrogates, and in cases when the surrogate regresses rather than interpolates (kriging with noise_variance not equal to zero, `linearSurrogate`, `SecondOrderPolynomialSurrogate`), we can check that that behavior also holds. I have added interpolation-checking tests to all surrogates except for Wendland, GEK, GEKPLS, and Earth. In testing, I found that Wendland doesn't seem to work at all, so that should be fixed.

## 4. Miscellaneous

I changed Kriging's default `p` from 2.0 to 1.99 to help numerical stability

## Still to do

- [ ] Add examples for kriging hyperparameter optimization to docs
- [ ] Add example for kriging noise variance to docs
- [ ] Fix Wendland surrogate
- [ ] Many doc examples are broken, would be good to fix {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/264","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/264/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/264/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/264/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/264","id":844056864,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU4NDQwNTY4NjQ=","number":264,"title":"Exponentially bad performance of radial basis polynomial basis evalution","user":{"login":"ChrisRackauckas","id":1814174,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4MTQxNzQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1814174?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas","html_url":"https://github.com/ChrisRackauckas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ChrisRackauckas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2021-03-30T04:25:52Z","updated_at":"2022-06-02T14:55:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```julia
"""
    multivar_poly_basis(x, ix, d, n)

Evaluates in `x` the `ix`-th element of the multivariate polynomial basis of maximum
degree `n` and `d` dimensions.

Time complexity: `(n+1)^d.`

# Example
For n=2, d=2 the multivariate polynomial basis is

1,
x,y
x^2,y^2,xy

Therefore the 3rd (ix=3) element is `y` .
Therefore when x=(13,43) and ix=3 this function will return 43.
"""
function multivar_poly_basis(x, ix, d, n)
        prod(
        a^d
        for (a, d)
        in zip(x, _make_combination(n, d, ix)))
end

function _make_combination(n, d, ix)
    exponents_combinations = [
        e
        for e
        in collect(
            Iterators.product(
                Iterators.repeated(0:n, d)...
            )
        )[:]
        if sum(e) <= n
    ]

    return exponents_combinations[ix + 1]
end
```

As the docstring says, `Time complexity: `(n+1)^d.` This is really bad. To avoid the issue in the linear radial basis function I can hardcode the solution, i.e.:

```julia
function Surrogates.multivar_poly_basis(x, ix, d, n)
    if n == 0
        return one(eltype(x))
    else
        prod(
        a^d
        for (a, d)
        in zip(x, Surrogates._make_combination(n, d, ix)))
    end
end
```

but this only fixes the degree zero case. This should be a priority to fix because this is the majority of the time consume in large RBF fitting. on from https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/issues/338, when we transferred all of our sampling methods over to `QuasiMonteCarlo.jl`, we encountered the issue that `QuasiMonteCarlo.jl` outputs samples as a $d\\times n$ `Matrix`, where $d$ is the dimension of the problem and $n$ is the number of samples, while `Surrogates` expects samples as a length-$n$ `Vector` of length-d `Tuples`. Converting `Surrogates.jl` to the former representation might improve speed and would allow us to remove the one remaining sampling-related bit of code, but would be a breaking change.
