behavior for the EI() optimizer seems to be finding the maximum of the function, not the minimum like the other optimizers. This fixes the behavior.


Testing code:

```julia

using Surrogates, LinearAlgebra, GLMakie

function test_surrogate()
    function branin(x)
        x1=x[1]
        x2=x[2]
        a=1;
        b=5.1/(4*π^2);
        c=5/π;
        r=6;
        s=10;
        t=1/(8π);
        (a*(x2-b*x1+c*x1-r)^2+s*(1-t)*cos(x1)+s)
    end
    n_samples = 50
    lower_bound = [-5.0, 0.0]
    upper_bound = [10.0, 15.0]

    xys = sample(n_samples, lower_bound, upper_bound, SobolSample())
    zs = branin.(xys);
    kriging_surrogate = Kriging(xys, zs, lower_bound, upper_bound, p=[1.9, 1.9])
    old_surr = deepcopy(kriging_surrogate)
    surrogate_optimize(branin, EI(), lower_bound, upper_bound, kriging_surrogate, UniformSample(), maxiters=10)
    return branin, old_surr, kriging_surrogate
end

let
    branin, old_surr, new_surr = test_surrogate()
    f = Figure()
    ax = Axis(f[1,1], xlabel = "x", ylabel = "y", title = "Points sampled by EI()")
    xs = LinRange(-5.0, 10, 100)
    ys = LinRange(0, 15, 100)
    fs = [branin((x, y)) for x in xs, y in ys]
    co = contourf!(ax, xs, ys, fs, levels = 30)
    Colorbar(f[1, 2], co)

    old_x, old_y = unzip(old_surr.x)
    npts = length(old_x)
    original = scatter!(ax, old_x, old_y, markersize=5, color = :black)
    new_x, new_y = unzip(new_surr.x[npts+1:end])
    newpts = scatter!(ax, new_x, new_y, markersize=10, color = :red)

    Legend(f[2, 1], [original, newpts], ["Original sample points", "New sample points"], tellheight = true, tellwidth = false, orientation = :horizontal)

    f
end
```

Current behavior (goes toward maximum)
![before](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/138738643-6b3d0782-649a-4124-adf7-2a2308166274.PNG)

Behavior of SRBF, for reference (correctly finds minimum)
![srbf](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/138738812-add7a42a-6f75-49ae-ad73-67cf7e370f3e.PNG)


Behavior with proposed changes (goes toward minimum)
![after](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/138738644-6289d1b3-f07b-42e0-806f-0b3a0a31066c.PNG)

function","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2021-10-25T17:02:00Z","updated_at":"2022-05-27T17:50:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pulls/303","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/303","diff_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/303.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Surrogates.jl/pull/303.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Current behavior for the EI() optimizer seems to be finding the maximum of the function, not the minimum like the other optimizers. This fixes the behavior.


Testing code:

```julia

using Surrogates, LinearAlgebra, GLMakie

function test_surrogate()
    function branin(x)
        x1=x[1]
        x2=x[2]
        a=1;
        b=5.1/(4*π^2);
        c=5/π;
        r=6;
        s=10;
        t=1/(8π);
        (a*(x2-b*x1+c*x1-r)^2+s*(1-t)*cos(x1)+s)
    end
    n_samples = 50
    lower_bound = [-5.0, 0.0]
    upper_bound = [10.0, 15.0]

    xys = sample(n_samples, lower_bound, upper_bound, SobolSample())
    zs = branin.(xys);
    kriging_surrogate = Kriging(xys, zs, lower_bound, upper_bound, p=[1.9, 1.9])
    old_surr = deepcopy(kriging_surrogate)
    surrogate_optimize(branin, EI(), lower_bound, upper_bound, kriging_surrogate, UniformSample(), maxiters=10)
    return branin, old_surr, kriging_surrogate
end

let
    branin, old_surr, new_surr = test_surrogate()
    f = Figure()
    ax = Axis(f[1,1], xlabel = "x", ylabel = "y", title = "Points sampled by EI()")
    xs = LinRange(-5.0, 10, 100)
    ys = LinRange(0, 15, 100)
    fs = [branin((x, y)) for x in xs, y in ys]
    co = contourf!(ax, xs, ys, fs, levels = 30)
    Colorbar(f[1, 2], co)

    old_x, old_y = unzip(old_surr.x)
    npts = length(old_x)
    original = scatter!(ax, old_x, old_y, markersize=5, color = :black)
    new_x, new_y = unzip(new_surr.x[npts+1:end])
    newpts = scatter!(ax, new_x, new_y, markersize=10, color = :red)

    Legend(f[2, 1], [original, newpts], ["Original sample points", "New sample points"], tellheight = true, tellwidth = false, orientation = :horizontal)

    f
end
```

Current behavior (goes toward maximum)
![before](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/138738643-6b3d0782-649a-4124-adf7-2a2308166274.PNG)

Behavior of SRBF, for reference (correctly finds minimum)
![srbf](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/138738812-add7a42a-6f75-49ae-ad73-67cf7e370f3e.PNG)


Behavior with proposed changes (goes toward minimum)
![after](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60799002/138738644-6289d1b3-f07b-42e0-806f-0b3a0a31066c.PNG)

QMC output samples as a d x n matrix, whereas surrogates expects them as a n-vector of d-tuples. I have it set up so that `Surrogates.sample` calls `QuasiMonteCarlo.sample` and then transforms the output to the desired shape:

```julia
using QuasiMonteCarlo
using QuasiMonteCarlo: SamplingAlgorithm

# We need to convert the matrix that QuasiMonteCarlo produces into a vector of Tuples like Surrogates expects
function sample(args...; kwargs...)
    s = QuasiMonteCarlo.sample(args...; kwargs...)
    if s isa Vector
        return s
    else
        _, npoints = size(s)
        s_reduced = [s[:, i] for i in 1:npoints]
        return Tuple.(s_reduced)
    end
end
```

 Also, `GoldenSample`, `KroneckerSample`, and `SectionSample` are not in `QuasiMonteCarlo.jl`, so I re-defined them as subtypes of `QuasiMonteCarlo.SamplingAlgorithm` and their sampling methods as instances of `QuasiMonteCarlo.sample`. Do we want to keep these in `Surrogates.jl` or move them entirely to `QuasiMonteCarlo.jl`?

I also made a small docs change and added some tests, but we might still need some more docs `QuasiMonteCarlo.jl` outputs samples as a d x n matrix, whereas surrogates expects them as a n-vector of d-tuples. I have it set up so that `Surrogates.sample` calls `QuasiMonteCarlo.sample` and then transforms the output to the desired shape:

```julia
using QuasiMonteCarlo
using QuasiMonteCarlo: SamplingAlgorithm

# We need to convert the matrix that QuasiMonteCarlo produces into a vector of Tuples like Surrogates expects
function sample(args...; kwargs...)
    s = QuasiMonteCarlo.sample(args...; kwargs...)
    if s isa Vector
        return s
    else
        _, npoints = size(s)
        s_reduced = [s[:, i] for i in 1:npoints]
        return Tuple.(s_reduced)
    end
end
```

 Also, `GoldenSample`, `KroneckerSample`, and `SectionSample` are not in `QuasiMonteCarlo.jl`, so I re-defined them as subtypes of `QuasiMonteCarlo.SamplingAlgorithm` and their sampling methods as instances of `QuasiMonteCarlo.sample`. Do we want to keep these in `Surrogates.jl` or move them entirely to `QuasiMonteCarlo.jl`?

I also made a small docs change and added some tests, but we might still need some more docs {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1647","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1647/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1647/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1647/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/OrdinaryDiffEq.jl/issues/1647","id":1197832670,"node_id":"I_kwDOBEVPe85HZXne","number":1647,"title":"Stochastic/occasional failure after a few timesteps","user":{"login":"archermarx","id":60799002,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwNzk5MDAy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60799002?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx","html_url":"https://github.com/archermarx","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/archermarx/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-04-08T21:08:11Z","updated_at":"2022-04-08T21:08:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi all,

I am working on a finite volume [simulation code](https://github.com/UM-PEPL/HallThruster.jl) which uses OrdinaryDiffEq for the timestepping. Overall, it works great. However, we have a problem where occasionally, without warning, the simulation will fail after a single timestep or a few timesteps. We will often have to rerun the same simulation with the exact same a few times in order to get one to complete. After this, it works fine for a while until we encounter this problem again and have to spam enter a few (1 - 15 or so) times until it runs. Predicting when this will happen or why it happens has eluded us so far, but today I was able to reproduce the problem with a minimum working example, where we solve linear advection on periodic boundary conditions:

Here's the MWE simulation code:

```julia
function step_function(;center = 0, width = 1, height = 1.0)
    step_func = x -> let center=center, width=width, height=height
        height * (abs(x - center) ≤ width/2)
    end
    return step_func
end

function solve_advection(end_time; ncells, nsave, advection_velocity, initial_condition = step_function(center = 0.5, width = 0.2), alg = SSPRK22())
    grid = LinRange(0.0, 1.0, ncells)
    saveat = LinRange(0.0, end_time, nsave)
    params = (;advection_velocity, grid)

    # The state matrix is set up this way in order to mimic the setup in HallThruster.jl, where we have some cells which
    # do not get updated by the OrdinaryDiffEq timestepping (boundary cells and the parts which we integrate implicitly)
    # Here, we have one state variable which represents the density of the advected quantity and which is updated by 
    # OrdiinaryDiffEq, and one which is untouched by OrdinaryDiffEq
    u = ones(2, ncells)
    u[1, :] = initial_condition.(grid)' |> collect
    u[2, :] = advection_velocity * ones(ncells)
    prob = ODEProblem{true}(update!, u, (0.0, end_time), params)

    dt = 0.9 * (grid[2] - grid[1]) / advection_velocity

    sol = solve(prob, alg; saveat, dt, adaptive=false)
    return sol
end

function update!(du, u, p, t)
    (;advection_velocity, grid) = p

    Δx = grid[2] - grid[1]

    for (i, x) in enumerate(grid)
        i_plus_1  = i == lastindex(grid)  ? firstindex(grid) : i+1
        i_minus_1 = i == firstindex(grid) ? lastindex(grid)  : i-1

        a⁺ = advection_velocity * (advection_velocity > 0)
        a⁻ = advection_velocity * (advection_velocity < 0)

        uL = u[1, i_minus_1]
        u0 = u[1, i]
        uR = u[1, i_plus_1]

        du[1, i] = - (a⁺ * (u0 - uL) / Δx + a⁻ * (uR - u0) / Δx)
    end

    return du
end

sol  = solve_advection(1.0; ncells = 100, nsave = 100, advection_velocity = 1, alg = SSPRK22())
```

This _almost always_ works. However, every so often it fails with `:Unstable` and we get the `Instability detected. Aborting` message. To better characterize this problem, I set up a stress test:

```julia
function stress_test(n, timesteps = 2; alg)
    advection_velocity = 5 * rand()
    ncells = rand(5:200)
    dt = 0.9 / ncells / advection_velocity
    count = 0
    time = timesteps * dt

    sol = solve_advection(time; ncells, nsave = timesteps+1, advection_velocity)

    sols = typeof(sol)[]
    fail_iters = Int[]

    for i in 1:n-1
        if sol.retcode != :Success
            count += 1
            push!(fail_iters, i)
            push!(sols, sol)
        end
        sol = solve_advection(time; ncells, nsave = timesteps+1, advection_velocity)
    end

    return fail_iters, sols
end
```

This generates random advection velocities and cell numbers and runs many identical simulations with these randomly-generated parameters, saving the solutions which fail and their corresponding iterations. This let me reproduce the problem a bit more consistently, as in a batch of 10000 simulations i would occasionally get one in which 3 or so failed, while most batches succeeded. Looking at the plots, it seemed that the problem lay in the second dummy state variable.

Interestingly, if change the following lines in the advection code:

```julia
u = ones(2, ncells)
u[1, :] = initial_condition.(grid)' |> collect
u[2, :] = advection_velocity * ones(ncells)
```

to

```julia
u = ones(1, ncells)
u[1, :] = initial_condition.(grid)' |> collect
```

the simulation doesn't fail ever. Similarly, if I manually set

```julia
du[2, i] = 0.0
```

the the simulation never fails. I had been assuming that if I don't set du, then du = 0 is assumed. This appears to be the case most of the time, but very rarely this doesn't seem to be true. I tested this with every third order SSP scheme, Euler(), RK4(), and Tsit5() and all exhibit this behavior. 

Proposed fix would be 
1) setting du .= 0 at the beginning, or
2) adding a warning in the documentation that you need to set dU = 0 manually if you have state variables which aren't changing (I know this isn't the best way to handle these problems, and I'm open to suggestions on how to better handle boundary conditions and the like).

I'm trying to mimic "delay" effect via ODE.
I checked that it works with Vector-form state.
However, with Matrix-form state, it does not work:
- example code
```julia
using DifferentialEquations
using LinearAlgebra


function test()
    n = 3
    Λ_func = t -> exp(-t)*ones(n) |> Diagonal |> Matrix
    τ = 0.2
    function dynamics!(du,u,p,t)
        du .= u - Λ_func(t-τ)
        nothing
    end
    x0 = zeros(n, n)
    M = zeros(n) |> Diagonal |> Matrix
    f = ODEFunction(dynamics!, mass_matrix=M)
    tspan = (0, 10.0)
    prob = ODEProblem(f, x0, tspan)
    sol = solve(prob)
end
```
- Result

```julia

using OrdinaryDiffEq
using LinearAlgebra

n = 3
Λ_func = t -> exp(-t)*ones(n) |> Diagonal |> Matrix
τ = 0.2
function dynamics!(du,u,p,t)
    du .= u - Λ_func(t-τ)
    nothing
end
x0 = zeros(n, n)
M = zeros(n*n) |> Diagonal |> Matrix
f = ODEFunction(dynamics!, mass_matrix=M)
tspan = (0, 10.0)
prob = ODEProblem(f, x0, tspan)
sol = solve(prob, Rosenbrock23())
